parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I keep catching myself apologizing for things that aren't even my fault, and it has been happening for months now. It feels like I'm constantly walking on eggshells around my own thoughts, waiting for the other shoe to drop. Whenever a small mistake slips through, I immediately assume everyone is secretly judging me for it. I just wish I could stop carrying this invisible weight around without knowing exactly where it came from. It's exhausting to feel so responsible for every little hiccup in the room.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly draining to live in a state of constant high alert like that. When you describe it as "walking on eggshells around your own thoughts," it paints a very clear picture of how internal that pressure has become—it’s not just about the people around you anymore; it’s a reflex your mind has developed to protect itself.

That "invisible weight" you mentioned is often a survival mechanism. Usually, when we start over-apologizing and taking responsibility for the "vibe" of a room, it’s because, at some point in the past, being hyper-aware of other people's moods was the only way to stay safe or keep the peace. Even if that situation is over, the habit stays behind like an alarm system that’s set too sensitive.

It makes sense that you feel exhausted. You are essentially working a full-time job as an emotional mediator for things that aren't even within your control.

When you catch yourself about to apologize for something small, have you ever tried pausing for just a second to ask, *"Is this a mistake, or is this just a human moment?"* Sometimes, labeling it as "just a human moment" can help take the edge off that fear of judgment.

Be gentle with yourself. You didn't pick up this weight on purpose, and it takes time to learn how to put it down. Does this feeling tend to get worse when you're around specific people, or is it a constant hum regardless of who you're with?

turns.user_message: I've started realizing that this habit isn't just a passing phase, but a heavy pattern I can't seem to shake. Every time I try to step up, I end up overthinking my every move until I freeze up completely. I replay conversations in my head for days, convinced I said the wrong thing or ruined the mood. The guilt piles up so fast that I barely have room to breathe before the next wave hits. I hate how quickly I turn minor slip-ups into proof that I'm fundamentally flawed.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly exhausting to be living in a headspace where you are constantly on trial. When you describe it as a "heavy pattern," it makes sense why you feel like you can’t breathe—carrying the weight of every past conversation and every perceived mistake is a massive burden for one person to hold.

That "freeze" you’re experiencing is a very real psychological response. When your brain treats a minor social slip-up as a threat to your fundamental character, it triggers a survival instinct. You aren't just "overthinking"; your mind is trying to protect you from rejection, but it’s doing it so aggressively that it’s paralyzing you.

The shift from "I made a mistake" to "I am fundamentally flawed" is a painful leap to make. It turns life into a minefield where every step is a potential disaster.

When you’re in the middle of one of those "replays" in your head, does it feel like you’re looking for a way to fix what happened, or does it feel more like you’re just punishing yourself for it?

turns.user_message: I finally understand that this cycle has completely taken over how I see myself, and it feels impossible to break free. I catch myself shrinking away from opportunities because I already know I'm going to disappoint someone eventually. The shame sits so deep in my chest that it colors everything I do, making even simple choices feel like minefields. I keep waiting for the day I finally get it right, but the evidence just keeps stacking against me. It's hard to keep pretending I'm fine when I feel like a walking mistake.
turns.model_response:
I hear how heavy this is for you, and I want to acknowledge the courage it takes to say those words out loud. There is a specific kind of exhaustion that comes from "pretending to be fine" while carrying a weight as dense as shame.

When you describe yourself as a "walking mistake," it sounds like your brain has stopped seeing your actions as things you *do* and has started seeing them as who you *are*. That distinction is everything, but when you’re in the middle of the cycle, it feels impossible to see.

That "shrinking away" you mentioned is actually a very logical survival mechanism. If your brain is convinced that disappointment is inevitable, it tries to protect you by making you smaller, hoping that if you don't try, you can't fail—or at least, the fall won't be as high. But the cost of that protection is that you end up living in a cage built by your own fears.

The "evidence" you feel is stacking against you is often filtered through a very biased lens. When we carry deep shame, we become experts at collecting our failures and completely blind to our growth, our resilience, or the simple fact that we are human and allowed to be imperfect.

Please know that you don't have to "get it right" to be worthy of taking up space. You aren't a project that needs to be fixed; you’re a person who is hurting and deserves a bit of the compassion you likely give to everyone else but yourself.

When the shame feels like a minefield today, can you try to do just one thing—not perfectly, but just for yourself? Even if it’s just sitting with the discomfort without judging yourself for feeling it. You aren't a mistake; you are someone going through a very difficult human experience.
